data.history:
The Al Dosari family descends from the ancient Dawasir tribal confederation that inhabited the Wadi al-Dawasir region of central Saudi Arabia. According to their genealogy, the Dawasir originate from a confederation of tribes including the Azd and Banu Taghlib (Banu Uqayl), with the 'original' Dawasir believed to descend from a military settlement (Doser) in Najd. Their name derives either from their purported forebear Dosser or from the Arabic word meaning 'soldiers,' with Wadi al-Dawasir itself meaning 'Steel Lion' according to some sources. The tribe has historically maintained exceptionally close ties with the Saudi royal family through strategic intermarriages, most notably between King Abdulaziz Al Saud and Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi of the Sudairi family, a respected Dawasir sub-clan. This marriage produced the famous 'Sudairi Seven' sons who became some of Saudi Arabia's most powerful figures, including Kings Fahd and Salman.

In Bahrain, the Dawasir settled mainly in Zallaq and Budaiya, becoming the second most powerful tribe after the Utub. They were so influential that they recognized Sheikh Isa Al Khalifa as ruler in name only and considered themselves exempt from taxation. In 1923, virtually the entire Dawasir tribe left Bahrain for Dammam, Saudi Arabia, opposing the British-supported succession of Sheikh Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa. They founded the cities of Dammam and Khobar in 1923 with King Abdulaziz's permission, initially hoping to return to Bahrain but ultimately establishing permanent settlements. The Dawasir were officially allowed to return to Bahrain in April 1927 after King Abdulaziz's intervention.

In Qatar, the tribe helped determine maritime borders through their fishing activities near the Hawar Islands, which became part of Bahrain partly due to Dawasir-Khalifa family connections. The research reveals a contemporary connection through Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani's third wife, Sheikha Noora bint Hathal Al Dosari, whom he married on 25 February 2014. They have four children together, including three sons and one daughter born between 2015 and 2020. The tribe also has historical presence in the UAE, particularly on Delma Island, Abu Dhabi, where Fahad Bin Rashid Al Dosari was a prominent pearl merchant.

Today, the Dawasir number over 6 million and remain one of the most powerful and influential tribes in Arabia. The family has produced notable contemporary figures including Ghanem Humood al-Masarir al-Dosari, a London-based Saudi human rights activist and political satirist who hosts the Ghanem Show and has been targeted by the Saudi government for his criticism of the royal family. He was granted political asylum in Britain in 2018 after facing harassment, physical attacks, and hacking attempts.
data.description: Al-Dosari is an Arabic surname representing a tribal family lineage with members spread across the Arabian Gulf states including Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Bahrain, descended from the powerful Dawasir tribal confederation.
data.originStory: The Al Dosari descend from the ancient Dawasir tribal confederation that inhabited the Wadi al-Dawasir region of central Saudi Arabia. During the 18th and 19th centuries, various family branches migrated eastward to establish trading settlements along the Arabian Gulf coast.
data.modernStatus: The Dawasir remain one of the most powerful and influential tribes in Arabia with over 6 million members. Contemporary figures include political activists and members connected to Gulf royal families through marriage.
data.nameEtymology: The name derives either from their purported forebear Dosser or from the Arabic word meaning 'soldiers,' with Wadi al-Dawasir itself meaning 'Steel Lion' according to some sources.

data.history:
The Al Naimi family name encompasses multiple distinct lineages across the Arabian Peninsula, most prominently the Na'im tribe and its various branches. The Na'im (Arabic: نعيم) is a traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribe that emerged as a dominant force in the United Arab Emirates region, with their traditional heartland centered around the Buraimi oasis and nearby Al Ain. According to British records from 1818, the Na'im numbered some 20,000 men in Buraimi at their peak, making them arguably the dominant force west of the Hajar Mountains. The tribe is divided into three main sections: Al Bu Kharaiban (from which the rulers of Ajman are drawn), Khawatir, and Al Bu Shamis, with the latter becoming virtually independent and closely associated with the Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi.

The tribe's expansion came primarily at the expense of the Dhawahir tribe, and they followed the Ghafiri tradition, supporting the Omani Imam Yarub in his bid to control Oman in 1723. Although initially linked to Wahhabi influence in the Buraimi area, the Na'im allied with Al Bu Falah ruler Khalifah bin Shakhbut in 1848 and Sultan Azzan bin Qais in 1869 to evict the Wahhabis from Buraimi, subsequently occupying many forts around the oasis. However, following the death of Zayed the Great, they came under Saudi influence again, leading to their participation in the Buraimi dispute of the 1950s.

The tribe experienced significant decline due to persistent internal divisions among sub-tribes, with their fighting strength dropping from 2,000 men in the early 20th century to just 300-400 rifles by the 1940s. During the Buraimi dispute in 1952-1955, some 200 Na'im, Al Bu Shamis, and Bani Kaab fought against British forces, with Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis assisting Saudi forces in occupying Hamasa village. Following the conflict, many Na'im were resettled in Saudi Arabia, while others settled in the Al Ain region of Abu Dhabi and the Omani Governorate of Al Buraimi.

The Al Naimi name also encompasses prominent individuals across different Gulf countries. Most notably, Ali bin Ibrahim Al-Naimi (born 1935) became Saudi Arabia's Minister of Petroleum and Mineral Resources from 1995 to 2016. Born in Ar-Rakah in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province to Ibrahim, a pearl diver of the Al-Naimi tribe, and Fatima, a Bedouin of the Ajman tribe, Ali Al-Naimi rose through the ranks of Saudi Aramco to become the first Saudi president of the company in 1983. His career exemplifies the transformation of Saudi Arabia's oil industry from foreign to indigenous control. In Qatar, the Al Naimi name is represented by figures like Huda al-Naimi, a medical physicist and writer, and other professionals across various fields, suggesting multiple distinct family lines bearing this name across the Gulf region.
data.description: Al Naimi represents multiple related entities across the Gulf: the Na'im Arab tribe prominent in the UAE and other Gulf countries, the Al Nuaimi ruling family of Ajman, and various prominent individuals in politics, academia, and business across Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Bahrain, and other Gulf states. The name is most notably associated with the traditional Na'im tribe's historical dominance in the Buraimi-Al Ain region and modern figures like former Saudi Oil Minister Ali Al-Naimi.
data.originStory: The Na'im tribe originated in the Arabian Peninsula with their traditional heartland being the Buraimi oasis and nearby Al Ain region. However, different Al Naimi lineages across the Gulf may have distinct origins - some are clearly from the Na'im tribe, while others may represent separate family lines that adopted similar names or have different tribal affiliations.
data.modernStatus: The Al Nuaimi family rules Ajman emirate in the UAE. Other Al Naimi individuals hold prominent positions across the Gulf, including former Saudi Oil Minister Ali Al-Naimi (1995-2016), various academics, medical professionals, athletes, and business figures in Qatar, Bahrain, and other Gulf states.

data.history: The Al Thani family has ruled Qatar as the sovereign dynasty since the mid-19th century, with their origins tracing to the Banu Tamim tribe of central Arabia's Najd region. According to historical records, they migrated from the Najdi town of Ushaiger, northwest of Riyadh, settling first at the Gebrin oasis in southern Najd before moving to Qatar around the 1720s-1740s under Thani bin Mohammed's leadership. Their initial Qatari settlement was in the southern town of Sikak, from where they moved northwest through Zubarah, Al Ruwais, and Fuwayrit before eventually establishing themselves in Doha in the 19th century. The family takes its name from Thani bin Mohammed, father of Mohammed bin Thani, who became the first recognized ruler in 1851. The family consists of three main branches: the Bani Ali, Bani Hamad, and Bani Khalid, with an estimated 20,000 members by the early 1990s. Unlike previous transitions involving bloodless coups, the most recent succession was peaceful when Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani abdicated in favor of his son Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani in June 2013. Under successive emirs, Qatar transformed from a pearl-diving economy to a major oil and gas producer, with the current emir Tamim continuing the family's political dominance while modernizing the country's economy and infrastructure. The family has navigated complex regional politics, including the 2017-2021 Gulf diplomatic crisis, while maintaining significant international influence through investments and diplomatic mediation efforts.
data.description: The Al Thani family is the ruling dynasty of Qatar, having governed the country since the mid-19th century and transforming it from a traditional pearling society into one of the world's wealthiest nations through oil and gas resources. The current emir, Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani, represents the eighth generation of Al Thani rulers.
data.originStory: The Al Thani originated from the Banu Tamim tribe in the Najd region of central Arabia, migrating from the town of Ushaiger northwest of Riyadh. They settled at the Gebrin oasis in southern Najd before moving to Qatar around the 1720s-1740s under Thani bin Mohammed, initially settling in Sikak before moving through various coastal towns and eventually establishing themselves in Doha.
data.modernStatus: Ruling family of Qatar with extensive global investments through the Qatar Investment Authority, owning stakes in major international companies including Barclays, Harrods, The Shard, Paris Saint-Germain F.C., and Volkswagen Group. Current emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani oversees one of the world's highest GDP per capita nations.

data.history: The Al-Noaimi (Al Nuaimi) family belongs to the Na'im tribe, a traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribal confederation with origins in the Buraimi oasis and nearby Al Ain region. The tribe's traditional heartland was contested territory, and their expansion came at the expense of the Dhawahir tribe. At their peak in 1818, the Na'im numbered around 20,000 men in Buraimi according to British records, making them arguably the dominant force west of the Hajar Mountains. The tribe was divided into three main sections: Al Bu Kharaiban, Khawatir, and Al Bu Shamis, with the Al Bu Kharaiban section eventually providing the rulers of Ajman. The Na'im followed the Ghafiri tradition and supported various political movements, including the Omani Imam Yarub in his bid to control Oman in 1723. The foundation of Ajman under Al Nuaimi rule occurred in 1816 when Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi and fifty of his followers took the coastal settlement of Ajman from members of the Al Bu Shamis tribe in a short conflict. The rule was endorsed by the Sheikh of neighboring Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah in 1816 or 1817. On 8 January 1820, following British naval action, Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid signed the General Maritime Treaty with the United Kingdom on 15 March at Falaya Fort. The Al Nuaimi rulers continued to govern Ajman through various treaties with the British, including the 1835 Maritime Treaty, the 1843 Maritime Treaty, and the 'Perpetual Treaty of Peace' in 1853. The family maintained control through the 19th and 20th centuries, with Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III serving as ruler from 1928 until 1981. On 2 December 1971, Ajman under Sheikh Rashid bin Humayd Al Nuaimi joined the United Arab Emirates as one of the seven founding emirates. The current ruler, Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III, has governed since 1981, with Sheikh Ammar bin Humaid Al Nuaimi serving as Crown Prince.
data.description: The Al-Noaimi (Al Nuaimi) family is the ruling royal family of the Emirate of Ajman, one of the seven emirates comprising the United Arab Emirates. They belong to the Na'im tribal confederation and have ruled Ajman continuously since 1816.
data.originStory: The Al Nuaimi branch of the Na'im tribe established rule in Ajman in 1816 when Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi and fifty followers conquered the coastal settlement from the Al Bu Shamis section of the same tribe in a brief conflict. Their rule was subsequently endorsed by the powerful Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah.
data.modernStatus: Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III currently rules the Emirate of Ajman (since 1981), with Crown Prince Sheikh Ammar bin Humaid Al Nuaimi as heir. The family oversees an emirate with a 2024 population of approximately 582,852 and GDP of US$4.23 billion as of 2012, contributing four seats to the UAE's Federal National Council.
data.nameEtymology: The family name Al-Noaimi is derived from the singular of 'Na'im', which refers to the major tribal confederation to which they belong.

data.history: The Nonoo family is a Jewish business family that originated from Iraq, specifically from Baghdad, and migrated to Bahrain in the late 19th century. According to family records, Houda Nonoo's grandfather Ibrahim left Baghdad in 1888 and established a financial business in Bahrain, founding the family's presence in the kingdom over a century ago. The family is part of the larger migration of Iraqi Jews, particularly from the Basrawi Jewish community, who moved to Bahrain in the 1880s seeking economic opportunities. The Nonoo family has maintained its Jewish identity while becoming deeply integrated into Bahraini society over more than 130 years. They have been prominent in business through the Basma Company, which offers various office services from IT to janitoring. The family has achieved remarkable political prominence despite being part of Bahrain's tiny Jewish community of around 30-37 people. Abraham Nonoo served as a member of the Manama Municipality in 1934, making him one of the first Jewish officials in Bahrain's political system. Ebrahim Daoud Nonoo became the first Jewish member of Bahrain's Shura Council between 2001-2006 and currently serves as CEO of the Basma Company and president of the Association of Gulf Jewish Communities. Most notably, Houda Ezra Ebrahim Nonoo served as Bahrain's Ambassador to the United States from 2008-2013, becoming the first Jewish ambassador of any Middle Eastern Arab country. The family has also produced successful entrepreneurs like fashion designer Misha Nonoo, who has gained international recognition and connections to British royalty. Despite the challenges faced by Bahrain's Jewish community, including riots in 1947-48 that forced many Jews to flee, the Nonoo family has remained and prospered, exemplifying successful integration while maintaining their distinct cultural and religious identity.
data.description: A prominent Jewish business and political family in Bahrain, originally from Iraq, known for producing the first Jewish ambassador of any Arab Middle Eastern country and maintaining significant business and political influence despite being part of Bahrain's tiny Jewish community.
data.originStory: The Nonoo family originated in Iraq, specifically Baghdad. Ibrahim Nonoo left Baghdad in 1888 and established a financial business in Bahrain, founding the family's presence in the kingdom. They were part of the larger migration of Iraqi Jews, particularly from the Basrawi Jewish community, who moved to Bahrain in the 1880s seeking economic opportunities.
data.modernStatus: The family operates the Basma Company offering office services from IT to janitoring, with Ebrahim Daoud Nonoo serving as CEO and president of the Association of Gulf Jewish Communities. Fashion designer Misha Nonoo has achieved international recognition and connections to British royalty, including collaborations with Meghan, Duchess of Sussex.

data.history: The Al Habtoor family has evolved into one of the UAE's most prominent business dynasties under the leadership of Khalaf Ahmad Al Habtoor. The family's business empire, the Al Habtoor Group, spans multiple sectors including construction, hospitality, automotive, and real estate development. Khalaf Ahmad Al Habtoor built an extensive international business portfolio, with notable ventures including Al Habtoor Motors (established 1983) serving as distributor for luxury automotive brands like Bentley, McLaren, and Mitsubishi. The family has also made significant international acquisitions, including the purchase of The President Abraham Lincoln Hotel in Springfield, Illinois for $9.3 million in 2014, marking their entry into the American hospitality market. In Dubai, the family has developed major infrastructure projects including the Al Habtoor Polo Club (opened October 2016) featuring world-class equestrian facilities with three full-size polo fields, one stick and ball field, two show jumping and dressage arenas, 520 stables, and five horse walkers. The massive mixed-use Al Habtoor City development comprises three luxury hotels (The St. Regis Dubai, The Westin Al Habtoor City, and W Dubai – Al Habtoor City) and three high-rise residential towers, with The St. Regis Dubai opening first in December 2015. Beyond business ventures, the family established the Khalaf Ahmad Al Habtoor Foundation in January 2013, conducting extensive humanitarian campaigns supporting Syrian and Palestinian refugees in Lebanon, distributing thousands of aid packages and supporting various Islamic educational and religious initiatives across the region.
data.description: The Al Habtoor family is a prominent Emirati business dynasty led by Khalaf Ahmad Al Habtoor, whose conglomerate spans construction, hospitality, automotive, and real estate sectors across the Middle East and internationally. The family operates major developments in Dubai including Al Habtoor City and the Al Habtoor Polo Club, while maintaining significant philanthropic activities through their foundation.

data.history: The Al Shamsi demonstrate a complex tribal and political history in the UAE, with the Al Bu Shamis tribe forming a distinct subsection of the Darawishah Na'im confederation. The Al Bu Shamis historically controlled significant territories including Al Heera (near Sharjah), Hamriyah, and parts of what later became Ajman emirate. In 1840, Sheikh Humeid bin Obeid bin Saeed Al Shamsi of Al Heera invaded neighboring Ajman but was eventually ousted by a joint force from Sharjah and Dubai, which burned Al Heera in retaliation, reflecting long-standing grievances between the Al Bu Shamis and the Al Bu Kharaiban Na'im of Ajman. The most prominent historical figure was Sheikh Abdulrahman bin Muhammad Al Shamsi, the influential and often controversial head of Al Heera township in the early 20th century, described by British officials as 'a stormy petrel of the Trucial Coast.' In June 1920, facing massive debts from failed pearling seasons, he led a desperate attack on Ajman fort, forcing Ruler Humaid bin Abdulaziz Al Nuaimi to flee, though British intervention ended his six-day occupation. His political influence extended through strategic marriages, with his daughters marrying Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi and his brother, and he played a crucial role in Sultan's successful 1924 campaign to retake Sharjah from Khalid bin Ahmad Al Qasimi. Despite being twice exiled by the British (to Aden 1925-1928 for alleged involvement in an assassination, and facing constant conflicts over slave manumission and territorial disputes), Abdulrahman remained a key power broker, even helping negotiate the establishment of Sharjah Airport in the 1930s. The Al Bu Shamis maintained semi-independence throughout the colonial period, often playing different powers against each other - including correspondence with Saudi authorities in the 1950s during oil exploration disputes. Contemporary Al Shamsi figures span diverse fields: Amna bint Abdullah Al Dahak Al Shamsi serves as UAE Minister of Climate Change and Environment since 2024, holding a PhD in Computer Engineering; Maitha Salem Al Shamsi serves as Minister of State and chairs multiple national committees; Abdul Aziz Nasser Al Shamsi has served as UAE Ambassador to Italy since 2008, previously holding ambassadorial roles in Brazil, Argentina, Chile, and France; and footballer Mohammed Al Shamsi plays as goalkeeper for Al Wahda FC.
data.description: The Al Shamsi are a prominent UAE family of Al Bu Shamis tribal origin, historically controlling territories around Sharjah and Ajman, who have evolved from influential tribal leaders and pearl traders to occupy significant positions in modern UAE government, diplomacy, and society.
data.originStory: The Al Shamsi trace their origins to the Al Bu Shamis tribe, a subsection of the Darawishah Na'im confederation. According to tribal genealogy, they descend from Shams bin Naim al-Khazraji from Mazen bin al-Azd, though alternative accounts suggest descent from Shams bin Amr al-Zahrani from Nasr bin al-Azd.

data.history: The Al Ameri family is a prominent merchant family with documented roots in the Bani Yas tribal confederation of Abu Dhabi. The family's most notable contemporary figure is Sheikh Mohammed Bin Musallam Bin Ham Al-Ameri, born January 11, 1972, in Al Ain, who has emerged as a billionaire businessman and former UAE Federal National Council member. His lineage traces through his father Sheikh Musallam bin Salem bin Ham Al Ameri and grandfather Sheikh Salem bin Ham, both of whom played formative roles in the early years of the UAE's development. The family's business empire centers around the Bin Ham Group, where Sheikh Mohammed serves as Deputy Chairman, having been appointed Vice Chairman in 2005. His educational background includes a master's degree from Grand Canyon University in the United States and a doctorate from the University of Kent in 2008. In 2011, he was elected to the Federal National Council, marking the family's formal entry into federal politics. The family has gained international recognition through Sheikh Mohammed's philanthropic commitments, notably joining The Giving Pledge alongside 215 other signatories from 24 countries to donate at least half his wealth to charity. He has also established himself as an author, publishing three books on UAE governance including 'United Arab Emirates - Wise Governance' (2012), 'The Shura in the UAE' (2015), and 'United Arab Emirates: The Road to Excellence' (2019). The family's influence extends beyond business and politics into sports, with footballer Zayed Al-Ameri representing the family name in the UAE Arabian Gulf League playing for Al Jazira club.

The family maintains significant influence in business, politics, and sports within the UAE.

data.history: The Al Zamil family traces its origins to the Anizah tribal confederation in Najd, settling in the Al-Ahsa oasis during the 18th century where they initially established themselves as date farmers and local traders before expanding into regional commerce. The family's modern business empire was founded in 1920 in the Qassim region by Sheikh Abdullah Al-Hamad Al-Zamil, who established what would become the Zamil Group Holding. Initially operating as a trade and services company until 1940, the business diversified into real estate and later expanded significantly into manufacturing and industrial services. The family's rise to prominence accelerated under the leadership of Abdulaziz bin Abdullah Al Zamil (1942-2019), who played a pivotal role in Saudi Arabia's industrial development as the original chief executive of Saudi Basic Industries Corporation (SABIC) from 1976-1983 and later as Minister of Industry and Electricity from 1983-1995. Under his guidance, SABIC became a major petrochemical conglomerate through strategic joint ventures with international partners, while the family's Zamil Group expanded into diverse sectors including steel manufacturing, air conditioning, offshore services, and petrochemicals. The group established Zamil Steel Company in 1976, Zamil Offshore Services in 1977, and later founded major petrochemical companies including Sipchem in 1999 and Sahara Petrochemical in 2004. Today, the Zamil Group employs over 12,000 people across 60 countries and represents one of Saudi Arabia's largest and most diversified business conglomerates, with several subsidiaries publicly listed on the Saudi stock exchange.
data.description: The Al Zamil family is a prominent Saudi Arabian merchant dynasty from the Eastern Province that has built one of the kingdom's largest business conglomerates, the Zamil Group, spanning manufacturing, construction, petrochemicals, and industrial services across the Gulf region. They are descended from the Anizah tribe and have been instrumental in Saudi Arabia's industrial development since the early 20th century.

data.history: The Abdul Latif Jameel family represents one of Saudi Arabia's most successful business dynasties, founded by Sheikh Abdul Latif Jameel (1909-1993) in Jeddah in 1945. Abdul Latif Jameel was the son of Hussein Jameel Mohammed Al-Saadi, indicating the family's Al-Saadi tribal origins. He had brothers Abdullah and Abdul Aziz, with Abdul Aziz being one of the founders of Al-Ittihad football club in Jeddah. Starting with simple goods purchased at public auctions in Jeddah's Islamic port alongside his friend Abdullah Hashem, Abdul Latif built a small car sales company that achieved a major breakthrough in 1955 when he secured the Toyota distributorship for Saudi Arabia, introducing the first four-wheel drive 'BJ' model from Toyota. He became the first to establish an automotive parts business in Mecca and expanded to secure agencies for global brands like Toshiba. Upon his death in 1993, his son Mohammed Abdul Latif Jameel assumed leadership as Chairman and CEO. The family business has grown into a diversified conglomerate operating across seven core sectors in over 30 countries. Mohammed's sons Fady serves as Vice Chairman International and Hassan as Vice Chairman of Saudi operations. Hassan Jameel, born 1979-1980, studied at Sophia University in Tokyo and London Business School, trained with Toyota in Japan, and became instrumental in the company's major investment in electric vehicle startup Rivian. The family has established significant philanthropic initiatives including Community Jameel (founded 2003), which supports the MIT Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab, MIT Jameel Clinic, and various educational and poverty alleviation programs globally. In September 2022, the company was ranked seventh in Forbes' list of the Middle East's Top 100 Arab Family Businesses.
data.description: Saudi business family founded by Abdul Latif Jameel (1909-1993), who established one of the largest Toyota distributorships globally and built a diversified conglomerate operating across 30 countries with seven core business sectors. The family ranked seventh among Forbes' Top 100 Arab Family Businesses in 2022.

data.history:
The Kamel family represents a branch within the extended al-Majid clan of Iraq's al-Bu Nasir tribe from the Tikrit region. The family patriarch was Kamel Hassan al-Majid (c. 1920-1996), son of Hassan Abd al-Majid and brother of the notorious Ali Hassan al-Majid ('Chemical Ali'). Kamel served as a Ba'ath Party official but was marginalized years before his death, living in relative obscurity compared to his more prominent relatives.

The family's prominence came through Kamel's two sons, Hussein Kamel al-Majid (1954-1996) and Saddam Kamel Hassan al-Majid (1956-1996), who became central figures in Saddam Hussein's regime through strategic marriages to the dictator's daughters Raghad and Rana respectively. Hussein Kamel rose through military ranks to become Supervisor of the Republican Guard in 1982, later serving as Minister of Industries and heading the Military Industrialisation Commission from 1987, where he supervised Iraq's chemical, biological and nuclear weapons programs. He also served as Oil Minister from 1990. Saddam Kamel served as head of the Republican Guard until 1986 and was Saddam's personal bodyguard, notable for his physical resemblance to the dictator, even portraying him in the propaganda film 'The Long Days'.

The family's downfall came dramatically in 1995 when both brothers, along with their wives (Saddam's daughters), defected to Jordan. They provided intelligence to UNSCOM, CIA and MI6 about Iraq's weapons programs, with Hussein Kamel confirming that Iraq had destroyed its entire stockpile of chemical and biological weapons after the Gulf War - information that was reportedly 'hushed up' because inspectors hoped to bluff Saddam into revealing more. However, after intermediaries assured them of pardons, the brothers returned to Iraq in February 1996.

Upon their return, they were immediately ordered to divorce their wives and were denounced as traitors. On 23 February 1996, three days after their arrival, they refused to surrender to Saddam's security forces and were killed in a 13-hour firefight at a safe house, along with other family members including Kamel himself, his son Hakim, daughter Ilham, and an unknown daughter. According to some accounts, they were killed by Ali Hassan al-Majid under house arrest. The firefight effectively ended the Kamel family's political influence and left them as a cautionary tale of loyalty and betrayal within Saddam's inner circle.

data.history: The Al Drees family has its documented origins with Sheikh Mohammed Saad Aldrees, who started his business career in 1938 (1356 Hijri) in the al-Artawiyah village of Saudi Arabia. Initially, he traded livestock including goats, sheep and camels, later expanding into transportation services for parcels and government mail. He purchased his first truck in the 1940s, establishing the foundation for what would become a major petroleum and logistics empire. In 1957, he opened his first gas station in Riyadh, which consisted of four barrels and one hose for supplying gasoline. On September 12, 1962, Sheikh Mohammed Saad Aldrees founded Mohammed Saad Aldrees and Sons Company Limited with his four sons as a family partnership company offering petroleum retailing and logistics services. In 1963, the company leased its first proper gas station in Riyadh at Rail Street, followed by another at Dhahran Street in al-Malazz neighborhood by 1965. The company became a limited liability company in 2002, and on December 14, 2004, was renamed Aldrees Petroleum and Transport Services Company (APTSCO) after its non-petroleum operations were spun off into a separate firm, Aldrees Industrial and Trading Company. The company went public on Saudi Arabia's Tadawul stock exchange between 2005-2006. As of 2021, Aldrees owned around 600 gas stations across Saudi Arabia, holding a 5.3% stake in the country's fuel station market, and has begun expanding internationally with bids to acquire gas stations in Egypt.
data.description: Al Drees is a prominent Saudi business family that built one of the Kingdom's largest petroleum retailing and logistics companies, APTSCO, which operates approximately 600 gas stations across Saudi Arabia. The family business, founded in 1962 by Sheikh Mohammed Saad Aldrees, has grown from humble livestock trading origins to become a publicly-traded multinational corporation with expansion plans in Egypt.
data.originStory: The Al Drees family's documented business origins trace to Sheikh Mohammed Saad Aldrees, who began as a livestock trader in al-Artawiyah village, Saudi Arabia in 1938. The family's rise from rural traders to petroleum industry leaders represents a typical trajectory of Saudi merchant families during the oil boom era.
data.modernStatus: The family controls Aldrees Petroleum and Transport Services Company (APTSCO), a publicly-traded multinational corporation listed on Saudi Arabia's Tadawul stock exchange. As of 2021, APTSCO operates approximately 600 gas stations across Saudi Arabia, holding 5.3% of the national fuel station market, and is pursuing international expansion including potential acquisition of 60 gas stations in Egypt.

data.history: The Boodai family is a prominent Kuwaiti merchant dynasty that has established significant influence across multiple business sectors in Kuwait and the broader Gulf region. The family's modern prominence centers around Boodai Corp, a family-owned conglomerate that serves as the parent organization for diverse business ventures throughout the Gulf countries. The company's flagship achievement is Al-Jazeera Airways, Kuwait's first private airline established in 2005, which ended the country's 50-year reliance on a single national carrier. Founded with an initial capital of $34 million (KD 10 million), the airline was established following the 2004 Emiree Decree #89 that liberalized Kuwait's aviation industry. The Boodai Group maintains substantial ownership in the airline through Wings Finance (9%) and Boodai Projects (17%). Beyond aviation, Boodai Corp owns Ramedia Group, which operates several newspapers and broadcasting channels including Al-Rai TV, as well as City Group Company, a transportation firm in Kuwait. The family has diversified into engineering, services, and cement production, demonstrating their broad business acumen. The family has produced notable figures across aviation, finance, media, and fashion sectors, with members taking leadership roles in major regional and international ventures. Marwan Marzouk Boodai serves as CEO and Chairman of Al-Jazeera Airways and Vice Chairman of Boodai Corp, while his brother Jassim Boodai operates as a journalist and owner of Al Rai Media Group. The family's influence extends beyond Kuwait through members like Fahed Faisal Boodai, who founded Gatehouse Financial Group and Gatehouse Bank in the UK, pioneering Islamic banking and real estate investment sectors. The family has also contributed to Kuwait's cultural landscape through figures like Nejoud Boodai, who was a prominent fashion designer and businesswoman before her death from cancer in 2010.

data.history: Based on the research provided, the Nass family represents distinct lineages with no apparent historical connections between documented branches. In the United States, the Nass family traces to German-Pomeranian origins through Friedrich Nass, who emigrated from the Pomerania region of the German Empire and settled in Walworth County, Wisconsin in the 1880s. This Germanic branch established itself in American politics through Stephen Leonard Nass, born October 7, 1952, in Whitewater, Wisconsin, the son of Wilfred Nass Sr., a longtime police officer, and Joyce (née Sherman). Stephen Nass has served in Wisconsin state government for over three decades, beginning with the Whitewater city council from 1977-1981, followed by 24 years in the State Assembly (1991-2015), and service in the Wisconsin Senate since 2015. He announced his retirement effective January 4, 2027. In Tanzania, a completely separate Nass lineage exists with William Nicholaus Lyimo, professionally known as Bill Nass, born April 11, 1993, to Chagga parents from the Kilimanjaro Region. This branch has achieved prominence in East African music, with Bill Nass becoming a notable rapper who founded his own record label, Mafioso Inc., in 2021. His single 'Puuh' featuring Jay Melody reached number one on Boomplay charts for six weeks and accumulated over 51 million streams. The research provides no evidence of any historical connection between these geographically and ethnically distinct Nass family branches.

data.history:
The Abida tribe is an ancient Arabian confederation of Qahtani origin with documented presence in the Arabian Peninsula since pre-Islamic times. According to genealogical records, they trace their lineage to the broader Qahtani tribal system and have been traditionally based in the central and southern regions of the Arabian Peninsula, with their ancestral homeland near Sarat Abida (also known as 'alhirja') near Thahran Al Janoub. The tribe's historical significance is greatly enhanced by their role as the parent tribe of the influential Bani Hajer (al-Hawajir), who emerged as a major sub-tribal confederation in the early 13th century under the leadership of Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, who led their migration from the Abida homeland to Lower Najd.

Under the subsequent leadership of Shafi bin Safar bin Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, the Bani Hajer migrated further eastward to the Al Ahsa'a region in the Eastern Province. The tribe achieved particular prominence during the early 20th century Saudi unification period, playing a decisive role in the Battle of Kinzan in Al Ahsa'a against the Al Ajman tribe in 1333 AH (1914-1915 AD). The Bani Hajer's intervention under Sheikh Shafi bin Salim Al Sahfi Al Hajri helped turn the tide in favor of King Abdulaziz Al Saud, who had been ambushed and wounded in the battle that also claimed his brother Sa'ad Bin Abdulrahman.

In recognition of their crucial support, King Abdulaziz granted the Bani Hajer the honorary title 'Khaznat Thafar' (Vault of Victory) and awarded them significant territorial rights from Ain Dar to the Qatar border, known as 'Jouf Bani Hajer,' along with certain judicial privileges including exemption from capital punishment and hand amputation for crimes committed by their tribesmen. The tribe is known for their trade activities with other tribes and for bringing foreign goods to Arabia. Today, descendants of the broader Abida confederation, particularly the Bani Hajer branch, are distributed across the GCC states with concentrations in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, Qatar, UAE, Bahrain, Kuwait, and Oman, where they are known by the surname Al-Hajri and continue to hold government offices and facilities in their settlements including Ain Dar, Salasil, Yakrub, Fouda, Al Sharmiah, Al Asidiah, Al Fardaniah, and other locations.

data.history:
The Al Bin Ali are a Sunni Arab sub-tribal confederation descended from the original Utub tribe, with the vast majority of members stemming from either the Bani Sulaim or Al-Maadeed tribes. Historical texts and maps indicate that over 1,500 years ago, the tribe originated from the center of Arabia – Najd, currently known as Riyadh. They trace their lineage to Ali bin Sulaiman, who established the family's presence in the Gulf region, making them part of the Adnani lineage through their Utub origins.

The Al Bin Ali settled at Zubarah in 1732, founding and ruling the town and its port, making it one of the most important port and pearl trading centers in the Persian Gulf in the 18th century. They expanded their settlements and constructed walls and a fort outside Zubarah called Qal'at Murair (Murair Castle), named after a water spring in the Bani Sulaim area next to Madina Al Munawara. The Al Bin Ali had practically independent status in Bahrain and Qatar as a self-governing tribe, using their distinctive Al-Sulami flag with four red and three white stripes with seven triangles facing west.

On 17 May 1783, war broke out between the Al Bin Ali Bani Utbah tribe and the army of Nasr Al-Madhkur. About 2,000 Persian troops attacked Zubarah, but after suffering defeat, the Persians withdrew. The strategist Shaikh Nasr Al-Madhkur's sword fell into the hands of Salama Bin Saif Al Bin Ali and was later given as a gift to King Abdul Aziz Al Saud. This victory was crucial in expelling the Persians from Bahrain after the Al Bin Ali's successful defense of their stronghold at Zubarah.

Sheikh Isa bin Hamad bin Tarif Al Bin Ali was chief of Al Bidda (modern Doha) and the Al Bin Ali tribe from the early 19th century until his death in 1847. Under his leadership, the tribe maintained significant political and economic influence. On 5 March 1837, the Al Bin Ali under Isa bin Tarif attacked Mombasa with help from Said bin Sultan of Oman, capturing Fort Jesus from the Portuguese on 12 March. According to J.G. Lorimer, the tribe owned upwards of 500 houses in Bahrain, concentrated in Muharraq and Al Hidd. A 1904 population estimate recorded 1,750 Al Bin Ali members in Qatar, most concentrated in Doha. The Al Bin Ali family became related by marriage to Bahrain's royal family and successfully fought off an attempted invasion by a maritime Al Binali force in 1895.

data.history: Al-Azaznah tribe was one of the tribal groups that exercised territorial control in the Al-Buraimi oasis region of eastern Arabia during the pre-Saudi era. According to historical records from the Al-Buraimi area, the tribe held authority over Al Hailah village within the broader Buraimi oasis system, which historically was known as 'Tawam' and 'Al Jaw' and consisted of nine villages. The oasis region was originally inhabited by Al Nuaimi tribal groups, with different families and tribes controlling various settlements - Al-Zar'a family governed Al-Buraimi proper, Al-Surur family held authority over Hamasah, Bani Jaber tribe controlled Sa'ara, and Al-Azaznah tribe ruled Al Hailah village. The tribe's political significance became evident during the complex power struggles of the region when they played a decisive role in local succession disputes. In a critical political betrayal around 1290 AH (approximately 1873 CE), Al-Azaznah tribe turned against Mohammed bin Ali Al Nuaimi, who had strong ties with the Bani Jaber tribe and had assumed leadership of the broader region. This betrayal led directly to Mohammed bin Ali's assassination, after which Al-Azaznah appointed themselves as rulers of Al-Buraimi, demonstrating their political ambitions and capacity for decisive action in the complex tribal politics of the eastern Arabian Peninsula. Their rule represents part of the intricate tribal governance system that characterized the Al-Buraimi oasis before the region came under broader imperial influences.

data.history: Al Fadl traces their lineage to Fadl ibn Rabi'ah ibn Hazim ibn Ali ibn Mufarrij ibn Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah, descending from the Banu Tayy confederation through the 10th-century Jarrahid rulers of Palestine. The tribe emerged as a major force in the 12th century, assisting the Burids and Zengids against the Crusaders in Syria. By 1107, Fadl was noted as an emir commanding mounted auxiliary troops for Damascus rulers. The Ayyubids appointed Al Fadl leaders to the prestigious office of Amir al-ʿarab, giving them command over northern Syria's Bedouin tribes. Under Mamluk rule from 1260, beginning with Emir Isa ibn Muhanna, the Al Fadl became hereditary holders of this office and received substantial iqtaʿat (fiefs) in Salamiyah and Palmyra. Their territory spanned from Homs westward to Qal'at Ja'bar eastward, and from the Euphrates valley north to central Arabia south. The Ottomans preserved their hereditary leadership after 1516, but by the mid-16th century, leading emirs joined the Mawali confederation and became known as Al Abu Risha, while their rivals were driven to the Beqaa Valley. During Ottoman times, the remaining Al Fadl split into Hourrouk and Fa'our branches, with the latter settling in the Golan Heights. The tribe became semi-sedentarized by the late 19th century but were displaced during the 1948 and 1967 Arab-Israeli wars, with most settling around Damascus. By the 1990s, an estimated 20,000-30,000 Al Fadl tribesmen lived in Syria and Lebanon, having transitioned from powerful medieval rulers to symbolic leadership.

data.history:
Al Janah is confirmed as one of the main branches of the powerful Bani Khalid tribal confederation that dominated Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. The Bani Khalid confederation consisted of multiple branches including Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir & Nahood, with the Al Humaid traditionally holding chieftainship. Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid in 1670, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces from Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions and established the Bani Khalid Emirate with its capital in Al-Mubarraz. The confederation controlled vast territories from the deserts surrounding Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif oases, wielding significant political influence across Eastern Arabia.

The Bani Khalid, including the Al Janah branch, maintained strong opposition to the emerging Saudi-Wahhabi alliance from the mid-18th century. When Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab sought support, the Khalidi chief ordered his expulsion, leading to lasting enmity with the Al Saud. Military confrontations intensified in 1763 with Saudi attacks led by Abdulaziz bin Muhammad, culminating in the Saudis deposing the Al 'Ura'yir rulers of Al-Hasa in 1789-1793. After brief Ottoman restoration in 1818, the Bani Khalid faced final defeats in battles against Mutayr-Ajman alliance in 1823 and Subay'-Saudi coalition in 1830, ending their political dominance.

Following their military defeats, many tribal members, including Al Janah families, dispersed across the Arabian Peninsula and Levant. Historical records indicate settlements in Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain, Syria, Palestine, and Lebanon. In central Syria, the village of Awja al-Janah was sold by the Bani Khalid to the Barazi family of Hama in the late 19th or early 20th centuries, demonstrating the far reach of the confederation's diaspora. The Al Janah branch, like other Bani Khalid segments, maintains its tribal identity within contemporary Gulf contexts while integrated into modern nation-states.

The Bani Khalid traditionally claims descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid, the renowned companion of Prophet Muhammad and military commander, though this lineage has been questioned by Arab genealogists. Alternative theories suggest descent from his relatives in Banu Makhzum or from other tribal origins including Banu Rabi'ah bin 'Amir bin Sa'sa'a from Hawazin, or Banu Ghazia from Tayy. The confederation included both Sunni and Shia Muslim members, with many adopting Shi'ite Islam during their rule in Eastern Arabia, contributing to their lasting conflict with the anti-Shi'ite Wahhabi movement.

data.history:
Al Mira was one of the two main branches of the Banu Rabi'ah, a sub-branch of the ancient Arab tribe Tayy (also known as Tayyids), alongside their kinsmen Al Fadl. The Al Mira descended from Mira (also spelled Al Murrah), son of Rabi'ah ibn Hazim ibn Ali ibn Mufarrij ibn Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah. The Banu Rabi'ah were descendants of the 10th-century Jarrahid rulers of Palestine and became prominent in Syria through their participation in the Muslim war effort against the Crusaders after 1099. By 1107, both Al Mira and their rival branch Al Fadl were noted as emirs providing mounted auxiliary troops for the Burid ruler of Damascus.

At some point during Ayyubid rule in the late 12th or early 13th century, Al Mira drove their kinsmen Al Fadl out of Hauran in southern Syria, forcing the latter to migrate north. This territorial conquest established Al Mira's dominance in southern Syria while their Al Fadl kinsmen controlled northern Syria. During the Mamluk period, Al Mira's territory extended from the Golan Heights (Jawlan) southward to the al-Harrah hot springs region north of Mecca in the Hejaz, giving them control over a vast stretch of southern Syria and western Arabia.

Under Mamluk rule, Al Mira's emir held the title of malik al-ʿarab (king of the Bedouin), a position that was lower in rank than the amir al-ʿarab title held by their Al Fadl kinsmen. Al Mira's strongest opposition came from their Al Fadl kinsmen under Ahmad ibn Hajji, creating a rivalry between the two branches of Banu Rabi'ah. However, this enmity gradually dissipated as Ahmad was given virtual independence in the southern desert while remaining under overall Mamluk suzerainty, allowing both branches to coexist within the broader tribal confederation networks that characterized the region.

data.history:
The Alhamamada are a Palestinian branch of the larger Mahamid tribe, an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb confederation. This places them within the Qahtani genealogical lineage, one of the two major Arab tribal divisions. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond.

According to tribal biography, when the Mahamid tribe enlarged and their areas of residence in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became too narrow to support the growing population, the tribe gathered to decide their future course. Some suggested conquering neighboring tribes, but this proposal was rejected because most of their neighbors were clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe, making them kinsmen. Instead, they chose migration, with those united by the fifth grandfather settling on leaving their ancestral lands.

The Mahamid migrated northward, first settling in the Dumat Al-Jandal area in the north for a period of time before moving toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region where they settled in such large numbers that they outnumbered the largest tribes in the region. In Karak, they allied themselves with local clans, most prominently Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily ruled by the Al-Amru clan. Their rule lasted for an extended period, though some Orientalists and historians recorded that they were harsh rulers, which eventually prompted different clans in the region to unite against them.

From their Levantine base, various branches of the Mahamid spread throughout the region. In Palestine specifically, multiple branches established themselves, including the Alhamamada along with other Palestinian branches such as the Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, Almusaeada, Alhasasana, Aljeayisa, Dar Alearabi, Al'asead, and Al'ahmad. The tribe's widespread distribution reflects their historical pattern of migration and settlement across the Arab world, with branches found from the Arabian Peninsula to the Levant, Iraq, and Arab Africa.

data.history: The Alsawalima are a Palestinian branch of the Mahamid tribe, which traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. When the Mahamid tribe grew too large for their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, tribal leaders convened to decide their future course. They rejected conquest of neighboring territories due to kinship ties with surrounding Harb tribe clans and instead chose migration as their solution. The Mahamid followed an extensive migration pattern northward, first settling in the Dumat Al-Jandal area before moving toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region where they established significant presence, growing so numerous that they outnumbered the largest local tribes. The Mahamid formed alliances with Karak clans, most notably Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established the Emirate of Mahamid under the leadership of the Al-Amru clan, which ruled for an extended period. From their Levantine settlements, particularly in Palestine, multiple branches emerged including the Alsawalima. The Palestinian branches of the Mahamid include kiawan, alkhudur, alsawalima, alhamamada, almusaeada, alhasasana, aljeayisa, dar alearabi, al'asead, and al'ahmad. The Alsawalima thus represent one of ten documented Palestinian branches that developed from the broader Mahamid tribal structure in the Levant.

Banu Asad (Arabic: بَنُو أَسَدْ) is an Adnanite Arab tribe descended from Asad ibn Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, tracing their lineage through Ishmael to Abraham. The tribe shares common ancestry with Prophet Muhammad through their ancestor Khuzaimah ibn Mudrikah, making them genealogical cousins of the Prophet. In the 6th century CE, Banu Asad rose to significant prominence when they revolted against the Kingdom of Kindah, killing King Hujr (father of the last Kindah king Imru' al-Qais) and ultimately causing the end of the Kingdom of Kindah, forcing Imru al-Qais to flee the Najd region.

The tribe produced notable literary figures including the illustrious Arabian mu'allaqat poet 'Abid bin al-Abras. In the 7th century, Banu Asad migrated from their original Najd territories to Iraq, settling primarily in Kufa near the banks of the Euphrates river, with additional communities established in Basra and Ahvaz alongside Banu Tamim. During the early Islamic civil wars, the tribe aligned with Ali ibn Abi Talib, participating in the Battle of the Camel and playing a crucial role in the Battle of Karbala (680 CE).

At Karbala, many prominent Banu Asad members died alongside Husayn ibn Ali, including Habib ibn Muzahir (commander of the left flank) and Muslim ibn Awsaja al-Asadi. Following the massacre, members of Banu Asad had the honor of burying the bodies of Husayn, his family, and companions three days later, earning them lasting respect among Shia Muslims. In 998 CE, Ali ben Mazyad of Banu Asad established the virtually independent Mazyadid emirate in the Kufa area, founding the city of Hillah in 1101 which became their capital. The Mazyadids wielded considerable influence for a century and a half through their powerful tribal army.

Today, Banu Asad members are distributed across multiple regions: Iraqi cities including Basra, Najaf, Kufa, Karbala, Nasiriyah, Amarah, Kut, Hillah, Diyala and Baghdad; Northern Sudan (Banu Kahil branch); Iran's Khuzestan province; Kuwait (Al Mazeedi family); Yemen (Hadhramaut); Oman; and India (Karnataka). The tribe maintains particular reverence among Shia Muslims for their role in burying Husayn's body and their martyrdom at Karbala.

data.history: The research provides very limited information about the Bani Abbad as an Arabian Peninsula tribal entity. The only concrete historical reference to Bani Abbad in the Arabian context appears in Ottoman court records from 1605, where they are documented as participating in a raid on the village of Beit Sahour near Jerusalem alongside the Ta'amireh, Bani Sakher, and al-Kilabina tribes of Transjordan. This record indicates that Bani Abbad was operating in the Transjordan region and was capable of coordinating military operations with other Bedouin tribes during the early 17th century. The raid on Beit Sahour was part of a series of Bedouin incursions that exploited weakened Ottoman authority in the Palestine-Transjordan region. Beyond this single documented event, the research provides no verified information about the tribal genealogy, origins, territorial control, or subsequent history of Bani Abbad in the Arabian Peninsula. The existing claims about descent from 'Abbad ibn Qays' and Adnani lineage cannot be confirmed through the available sources, and the relationship between this Transjordanian Bani Abbad and other groups bearing similar names remains unclear from the research.

This attack was part of Bedouin incursions exploiting weakened Ottoman authority in the region.

data.history:
The Bani Kaab (Arabic: بني كعب, singular Al Kaabi الكعبي) is an Arab tribe with origins tracing to the Najd region, specifically descended from the ancient Banu Amir confederation through the lineage of Kaab bin Rabia bin Aamer bin Sa'sa'ah. The tribe comprises numerous subsections including the Drisah, Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Shwaihiyin, Yidwah and Zahairat. By the turn of the 20th century, most sections had settled with a total population of approximately 7,250, of whom 1,150 remained Bedouin. Only the Drisah and Shwaihiyin subsections maintained nomadic lifestyles while other sections had adopted settled life in areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and territories in the Emirates east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor.

Historically, the Bani Kaab engaged in the complex tribal politics of southeastern Arabia, forming shifting alliances based on political and economic interests. By 1844, they allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation aimed at expelling Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. Despite this alliance, they maintained rivalry with the Na'im and Bani Qitab tribes, successfully pushing sections of the Na'im northward to the Jiri plain. A century later, demonstrating the fluid nature of tribal allegiances, the Bani Kaab had aligned with Saudi Arabia by the 1940s, with their Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.'

Interestingly, traditional rivals Na'im and Bani Kaab temporarily united in the 1940s when oil concessions became a possibility, opposing oil companies, the Trucial Sheikhs, the Sultan of Muscat, and British authorities alike. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Bani Kaab supported the Saudi incursion led by Shaikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and actively fought against British forces, specifically the Trucial Oman Levies. In the final military action at Buraimi in October 1955, a Bedouin force of some 200 men from the Al Bu Shamis and Bani Kaab under Sheikh Obaid bin Juma put up spirited resistance to the British-backed Trucial Oman Levies, demonstrating their continued martial capabilities and loyalty to their Saudi allies.

The broader Banu Ka'b confederation, of which the Gulf Bani Kaab represents one branch, had migrated from the Najd region in multiple waves since the 6th century AH due to Islamic conquests, drought, and tribal conflicts. Different branches settled across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond - some went to Iraq (eventually converting to Shia Islam), others to northern Morocco, and still others to the mountains and coastal areas of Oman and the UAE where they maintained Sunni Islamic practices. The tribe has produced notable modern figures including Noura bint Mohammed Al Kaabi (UAE Minister of State), Saad Sherida al-Kaabi (Qatar's Minister of Energy), and various professional athletes and officials across the Gulf states.

data.history: Banu Adi was a prominent clan of the Quraysh tribe, descended from Adi ibn Ka'b ibn Lu'ayy ibn Ghaleb ibn Fihr. Based in pre-Islamic Mecca, they were part of the al-Ahlaf ('the Confederates') faction alongside the clans of Abd al-Dar, Banu Sahm, Banu Makhzum and Banu Jumah, in rivalry to the al-Mutayyabun faction that included Banu Hashim. The tribe's most significant historical contribution was producing Umar ibn al-Khattab, the second Rashidun Caliph (634-644 CE), whose leadership transformed the early Islamic state. Other notable members included Zayd ibn al-Khattab (Umar's brother who died in 632), Sa'id bin Zayd (a companion of Muhammad who died in 671), and the pre-Islamic monotheist Zayd ibn Amr (died 605). The clan also produced Hafsa bint Umar, who became a wife of Prophet Muhammad, and Al-Shifa' bint Abdullah, noted as the only literate female sahaba. During the early Islamic conflicts, Banu Adi was with the Meccan escort that preceded the Battle of Badr but notably did not join the main Quraysh force in the battle itself. As part of the broader Quraysh confederation, they eventually converted to Islam when Muhammad conquered Mecca in 630 CE and became integrated into the early Islamic elite. The tribe's prominence was primarily tied to their Meccan origins and their role in early Islamic history rather than later tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula.

data.history: The research provided contains no information about the Banu Kathir tribe. The Wikipedia articles focus entirely on Jewish Arabian tribes of early Islamic Arabia - specifically the Banu Qurayza, Banu Nadir, and Banu Qaynuqa who lived in Medina during Muhammad's time (7th century CE). These Jewish tribes were involved in conflicts with the early Muslim community and were ultimately expelled or eliminated from Medina between 624-627 CE. One Arabic Wikipedia entry discusses the Kathiri Sultanate, which was established in 1379 in the Hadhramaut region and ruled until 1967, but this appears to be a different political entity rather than the tribal origins of Banu Kathir. The research also includes general information about Kahlan tribal confederations and mentions that 'Banu Kathir moved to Hadramut in the East of Yemen where they established their own sultanate' as part of Hamdan branches, but provides no specific details about their genealogy, migrations, or tribal structure. Without research material directly addressing the Banu Kathir tribe's origins, genealogical connections, or historical development, no verified information can be provided about their tribal history.

Banu Muayiya was a branch of the Kindah tribe, which formed part of the larger Kahlan tribal confederation descended from Qahtani origins in ancient Yemen. The Kindah originally dwelt in Bahrain but were expelled to East Yemen, from where a group moved to Najd and established a powerful vassal kingdom under Himyar. As one of the main Kindite branches, Banu Muayiya ruled much of northern Arabia and Bahrain, maintaining their political power through direct affiliation with the Himyarite kingdom.

The tribe's prominence was intrinsically tied to their Himyarite patrons, and they experienced significant decline after Himyar's fall in 525 AD. Following the collapse of Himyarite authority, Kindite towns controlled by Banu Muayiya and other Kindite branches fell under constant Bedouin raids from Najd. These persistent attacks eventually destroyed the Kindite presence in their territories, with survivors being absorbed into the Najdi tribal federations.

Banu Muayiya's territorial influence extended across northern Arabia and into Bahrain, where they maintained control as part of the broader Kindite political structure. Their decline represents part of the larger pattern of Kindite dissolution that occurred throughout the 6th century AD, as the various Kindite branches lost their political cohesion and territorial control following the end of Himyarite support. This places Banu Muayiya firmly within the Qahtani-Kindite genealogical framework rather than any Adnani lineage.

The Banu Daws (Arabic: بنو دوس) was an ancient Arabian tribe from the Azd confederation, historically located in the mountainous regions south of Mecca in the Tihama region. According to genealogical sources, Daws traces its lineage to Daws ibn Udathah, a descendant of Zayd al-Lat from the Azd tribal confederation, establishing them firmly within the Qahtani lineage through their connection to the Zahran tribe. Abu Hurairah's full genealogy confirms this lineage as 'Abd al-Raḥmān ibn Ṣakhr al-Dawsī al-Zahrānī, with Al-Dhahabi noting he was born in the Al-Bahah region and Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani tracing the Banu Daws lineage through Zahran to Azd, a Nabatean ancestor of the southern Arabs.

The tribe gained prominence in Islamic history through two of its most distinguished members: Tufayl ibn Amr, who served as their chief and was an early convert to Islam around four years before the hijra in 622 CE, and Abu Hurairah, who became one of the most prolific hadith narrators in Islamic tradition. Tufayl's conversion story is particularly significant - when he traveled to Mecca for Hajj, he was warned by the Meccans about Muhammad's supposed magical powers and even placed cotton wool in his ears to avoid hearing him. However, during his circumambulation, he encountered Muhammad reciting the Quran and, reasoning that as an intelligent tribal leader he should be able to resist any magical influence, he removed the cotton wool, listened to the Quran, and ultimately converted to Islam.

During the early Islamic period, the Daws played an active military role under Tufayl ibn Amr's leadership. He led a contingent of his people against the false prophet Musaylima during the Ridda wars, ultimately falling as a martyr in the Battle of Yamama in 633 CE. Abu Hurairah's career was equally distinguished - after converting to Islam through Tufayl in 629 CE (7 AH), he became one of the inhabitants of the Suffah in Medina and accompanied Muhammad on various expeditions. Under the Rashidun caliphs, he served as a muezzin to al-Ala al-Hadhrami in Bahrain and briefly served as governor of Bahrain during Umar's reign, though he later declined Umar's offer to serve again in that capacity.

The tribe's pre-Islamic religious practices centered around the worship of Dhu al-Khalasah, an idol housed in a Kaaba-style structure. This practice is referenced in Islamic eschatological prophecies, with Abu Hurairah narrating a hadith from Bukhari about the end times that mentions 'the buttocks of the women of Daws moving while going around Dhu al-Khalasah.' According to historical sources, this Kaaba was later converted into a mosque following the tribe's conversion to Islam. The Daws were regarded favorably by Muhammad, who viewed them on par with his own tribe of Quraysh, the Ansar of Medina, and Banu Thaqif, reflecting their respected status within the broader Arabian tribal confederation.

The Fad'an are a major branch of the Anizah tribal confederation, tracing their genealogy through the Adnani Arab lineage alongside the tribes of Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib within the large Rabiʿa branch. The Anizah tribe's existence as an autonomous tribal group predates Islam, with two main branches recorded by early historians - one nomadic in northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and another sedentary in the wadis of Al-Yamama in eastern Najd. During the Ottoman era, the Anizah became masters of the oasis towns of northwestern Arabia, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, spending winter months in these areas while migrating northwards into southern Syria during summer months.

The Fad'an entered the Syrian plains in the early 19th century as part of the broader Anizah expansion into the Levant. The first Anizah tribe to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, establishing dominance around Hama and Homs. The Fad'an, along with their subordinate tribe the Sba'a, began challenging the established Hasana control over the plains east of Hama and Homs in the early 19th century. In 1814, large numbers of Fad'an and Sba'a forces entered the plains east of the Orontes River, prompting the Hasana to ally with local Ottoman troops against them. Despite initial resistance lasting two weeks, the Fad'an eventually retreated but continued to return periodically to raid Hasana territories west of Hama.

The Fad'an's territorial influence extended significantly during the Ottoman period, as they displaced established tribes and reshaped the tribal geography of central Syria. Following the Mawali tribe's devastating defeat in 1786 and their displacement by Anaza tribes, the Fad'an were among those granted the former Mawali rights to collect tolls from caravans traversing through Palmyra. The Hadidiyin tribe migrated from areas near Aleppo and the Euphrates valley to the Hama region in the mid-18th century specifically due to pressures from the advancing Fad'an, demonstrating their significant military capacity.

Within the broader Anizah structure, the Fad'an maintained their own sheikhs, with notable families including Ibn Ghabin (Sheikh of Dana Kahil from Al-Fadaan), Ibn Muhaid (Sheikh of Al-Fadaan), Ibn Quaishish (Sheikh of Al-Kharsa and currently sheikh of Dana Majid from Al-Fadaan), and Ibn Huraymis (Sheikh Al-Aqaqrah from Al-Fadaan). Today, settled Fad'an families are found not only in Saudi Arabia, but also in Kuwait, Jordan, Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Oman, Bahrain, and other regional states, often distinguished by retaining tribal appellations as surnames following the establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the 20th century.

data.history: The Guzayah represents a crucial ancestral branch in Arabian tribal genealogy, serving as the direct ancestor of the major Otaibah tribe. According to 8th-century historian Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's manuscript 'The Great Ancestry', verified in 1988, Guzayah was the son of Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin, placing the tribe within the broader Hawazin confederacy that traces back through Qays ʿAylān to the Adnanite lineage. Al-Kalbi's work 'The Abundance of Kinship' specifically identifies Guzayah as one of three sons of Jusham (alongside Oday and Ouseema), and notes that 'The sons of Guzayah are Juda'aa, Hami, Otaibah, and Outwara.' This genealogical position makes Guzayah the father of Otaibah, whose descendants would become one of the largest Arabian tribes. The Guzayah lineage extends back through the classical Arabian genealogical chain: Hawāzin, Manṣūr, ʿIkrima, Khasafa, Qays ʿAylān, Mudar, Nizar, Ma'add, and ultimately to Adnan of the Ishmaelites, following the Abrahamic tradition that Arabs are descendants of Abraham through his son Ishmael. The Hawazin confederacy, of which Guzayah was part, was among the major tribal groupings of pre-Islamic Arabia mentioned in the research as having descendants throughout Arabia, Libya, Algeria, Morocco, Sudan, and Iraq. While Guzayah itself appears to have been absorbed into or superseded by its descendant tribes, particularly the Otaibah, its genealogical significance remained preserved in medieval Arabic historical sources. The tribe's legacy continued through its descendants who would play major roles in Arabian Peninsula history, from pre-Islamic times through the formation of modern Saudi Arabia, as the Otaibah tribe became one of the most prominent and numerous tribes in the region.

The Hasana tribe represents a branch of the Anaza tribal confederation, originating from the broader Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. The Aniza confederation's genealogical roots trace back to pre-Islamic Arabia, with the classical Arab genealogists placing them within the large Rabiʿa tribal structure. The Hasana became historically significant as the first Anaza tribe to migrate from their traditional territories in Najd to Syria, arriving in 1757 and establishing themselves in the plains east of Hama and Homs. Under their leader Muhanna al-Fadil, the Hasana drove out the long-established Mawali tribe from their traditional pasture grounds by the late 1780s, becoming the dominant Bedouin force in central Syria. The tribe gained Ottoman government sanction to collect tolls from caravans on the Palmyra trade routes and collected tribute (khuwwa) from weaker tribes and villages in the region. Despite their dominance, the Hasana faced territorial challenges in the early 19th century from other Anaza branches, particularly the Fad'an and their subordinates the Siba'a, leading to significant conflicts over control of the plains east of the Orontes River. During the French Mandate period (1920-1946), the Hasana leadership under Trad al-Milhim received substantial landholdings, with some twenty villages registered in his name during the 1930s, though this made him dependent on government support and diminished his traditional tribal authority. Following Syrian independence, the tribe continued to inhabit villages east of Homs and Hama with their headquarters in Buwaydah, adapting to the modern political system through parliamentary representation, with the al-Milhim family gaining seats in 1977 and 1994 under Hafez al-Assad's government and maintaining loyalty to the Syrian government throughout the Syrian civil war.

data.history:
Jadis was an ancient Arabian tribe that, according to Arab genealogical tradition, descended from either Lud or Aram, both sons of Shem (Sam), son of Noah, though the 11th-century historian Miskawayh stated that both Jadis and their neighbors Tasm were sons of Lud. The tribe was classified among the al-Arab al-Ba'ida (extinct Arabian tribes) and belonged to the Qahtani lineage of South Arabian tribes. They settled in the region of Al-Yamama in the Arabian Peninsula alongside the Tasm tribe, where they built palaces and fortresses and created an independent kingdom and civilization that flourished from approximately 110 BCE until their extinction in the 6th century CE.

By the 6th century CE, both Jadis and Tasm were ruled by King Imliq of Tasm, who had implemented oppressive policies including droit du seigneur, deflowering Jadis virgins before their marriage, and controlling trade routes. The Jadis eventually rebelled against this tyranny and assassinated King Imliq during a special performance, where several men from the Jadis revealed hidden swords from the sand and killed him. This act of rebellion would ultimately lead to their destruction.

Despite constant warnings from Zarqa al-Yamama, a legendary blue-eyed woman of their own tribe who possessed exceptional eyesight and could reportedly see enemies from a week's distance, the Jadis ignored the threat of incoming Himyarite armies. The Himyarite forces entered Al-Yamama and completely exterminated the unprepared Jadis tribe, killing all adults and enslaving the children in retaliation for the assassination of King Imliq, who was an ally of Himyar. This genocide marked the complete end of Jadis as a distinct tribal entity.

After their extinction, the palaces and fortresses that Jadis had built in Al-Yamama were taken over and used by the Banu Hanifa tribe. The tribe's legacy survived primarily in Arabian oral traditions and genealogical literature rather than detailed historical records. Their story was later adapted into the 2005 Jordanian television series 'Akher Ayyam Al Yamama' (The Last Days of Al Yamama), demonstrating the enduring cultural memory of these ancient Arabian peoples.

folkLegends.title: The Legend of Zarqa al-Yamama
folkLegends.story: Zarqa al-Yamama was a legendary blue-eyed woman from the Jadis tribe who possessed exceptional intuition, sharp eyesight, and the ability to predict events before they occurred. She was believed to have the ability to see riders from the distance of one week. When enemies of her tribe decided to hide behind trees which they carried to evade her gaze, Zarqa noticed the moving trees and alerted her tribe that the trees were moving towards them. However, members of her tribe thought she was going mad and chose to ignore her warning. The troops of Hassan al-Himyari eventually reached her tribe and killed every man in the camp, then they tore out Zarqa's eyes and crucified her.

folkLegends.title: Migration from Hejaz Legend
folkLegends.story: According to legend, the Banu Hanifa tribe moved to al-Yamama from the Hejaz after the region's original inhabitants, the extinct people of Tasm and Jadis, were decimated by war.

data.history:
Kahlan (Arabic: كهلان) was one of the main tribal confederations of the ancient Sabaean kingdom in Yemen, descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, making them part of the greater Qahtanite Arab lineage. Originally dominant in the Yemeni highlands around the ancient capital of Marib, the Kahlani tribes controlled much of the territory in the Sabaean heartland. By the 2nd century BC, the declining Sabaean state faced pressure from the rising Himyarite kingdom in the southern lowlands, which successfully allied with nomadic tribes and gradually incorporated Saba into their domain, forcing the resistant Kahlan tribes out of the Yemeni highlands.

Most Kahlani groups retreated to the desert regions around Marib, where they remained until the catastrophic destruction of the great Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD. This pivotal event triggered a massive northward migration of Kahlani tribes throughout the Arabian Peninsula, with various branches reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria before the 7th century Islamic conquests. The confederation branched into five major tribal groups: Azd, Hamdan, Lakhm, Tayy, and Kinda, each following different migration routes and establishing distinct settlements across the Arabian world.

The Azd branched into four main divisions in the 3rd century AD, each led by sons of the Arabian king Muzayqiya. Imran bin Amr led the bulk of the tribe to Oman, establishing the Azdi presence in Eastern Arabia and later invading Karman and Shiraz in Southern Persia. Jafnah ibn Amr headed for Syria where he established the Ghassanid kingdom, named after a spring where they stopped. Thalabah bin Amr settled in the Hijaz near Yathrib, becoming the ancestor of the Aws and Khazraj tribes who would later become the Ansar in Islam and produce the Nasrid dynasty in Spain. Haritha bin Amr wandered in the Hijaz until reaching Tihama, fathering Uday (ancestor of Bariq) and Lahi (ancestor of Khuza'a).

The Hamdan branch remained primarily in Yemen, with Hashid and Bakil staying in the highlands north of Sana'a between Marib and Hajja. Banu Yam settled north of Bakil in Najran and later branched into Al Murrah and 'Ujman tribes of eastern Saudi Arabia. Other Hamdan branches included Banu Kathir who moved to Hadramaut and established their own sultanate, and Banu Al-Mashruki who settled in Lebanon. The Lakhm spread northward to Southern and Western Mesopotamia, becoming the first Southern Arabs to settle Northern Egypt. The Tayy, led by Usma bin Luai around 115 BC, invaded the mountains of Ajaa and Salma in northern Arabia, later changing their name to Shammar. The Kinda dwelt in Bahrain but were expelled to East Yemen, establishing a vassal kingdom for Himyar in Nejd that gradually declined after Himyar's fall in 525 AD.
