data.history:
The Kinana tribe (كنانة) is one of the most prestigious Arab tribes of Adnani lineage, tracing their ancestry to Kinana ibn Khuzayma ibn Mudrika ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, ultimately descending from Ishmael. Their traditional territory extended from the Tihama coastline near Mecca northeastward to the borders of their tribal relatives, the Banu Asad. The tribe consisted of six principal branches: al-Nadr (who became the parent tribe of Quraysh), Malik, Milkan, Amir, Amr, and Abd Manat. The Abd Manat included particularly strong subgroups like Bakr ibn Abd Manat, whose main branches were the Mudlij, Du'il, Layth, and Damra. The Ghifar subgroup belonged to the Damra, while the Harith ibn Abd Manat formed the core of the Ahabish confederation.

Kinana played a crucial role in Arabian history as the parent tribe of Quraysh, the tribe of Prophet Muhammad. Their ancestor Fihr ibn Malik emerged as leader of Kinana in their victory against a branch of the Himyarites, and his descendant Qusayy ibn Kilab was backed by Kinana in capturing Mecca. During the pre-Islamic period, the tribe was involved in the Fijar Wars, where various Kinana subgroups participated on different sides - the main tribe supported the Quraysh against the Hawazin confederation, though internal tensions existed, particularly between different branches over tribal leadership and territorial control.

During the early Islamic period, various Kinana subgroups had different responses to Islam. While Quraysh initially opposed Muhammad, other Kinana branches like the Ghifar, Layth, and Damra supported his conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. Following the Muslim conquests, Kinana became one of the main components of the Arab tribal garrison of Jund Filastin (Palestine), alongside Judham, Lakhm, Khuza'a, Khath'am, and Azd. Caliph Umar appointed two Kinani governors over Palestine around 638-639 CE.

The tribe maintained significant presence in Palestine until the Crusades, when their emirs fled to Fatimid Egypt in 1153 CE and were resettled in Damietta as the Kinaniyya. Under the Ayyubids, they served as second-tier troops and fought against the Crusaders. During the Seventh Crusade in 1249 CE, the Kinani garrison of Damietta fled at the sight of Louis IX's forces, leading to executions of their commanders. Today, Kinana descendants are found throughout the Arab world, with major concentrations in their original Hijazi territories around Mecca and the Tihamah coast, as well as in Iraq, Jordan, Sudan, Egypt, Palestine, and other regions where they migrated during the Islamic conquests.

folkLegends.title: Victory Against the Himyarites
folkLegends.story: Fihr ibn Malik defended the city of Mecca from the Himyarite Kingdom, who intended to take away the stones of the Kaaba and transport them to Yemen so the Hajj would be transferred there instead. Fihr led a joint force of Quraysh and warriors from the Arabian tribes of Mudar, Banu Kinana, Banu Asad, Banu Hudhayl and Banu Tamim which repelled the Himyarites. The enemy general, Hassan ibn 'Abd-Kulal was captured and imprisoned for three years until he was able to ransom himself out of custody.

data.history:
Madhhij was one of the major ancient Qahtanite Arab tribal confederations that originated in southern Arabia, specifically Yemen and parts of Najran. The confederation descended from the eponymous ancestor Madhhij, son of Adad ibn Zayd ibn Yashjub ibn Urayb ibn Zayd ibn Kahlan ibn Saba, establishing their clear Qahtanite lineage. Al-Hamdani's 10th-century work 'Description of the Arabian Peninsula' cited Madhhij thirty times, documenting their major branches including Nukha, Zubaid, Ruha, and Hada - the latter renowned as the best archers among the Arabs. The tribe is documented in early historical sources, including the Namara inscription of 328 CE, which records that the Nasrid king of al-Hira, Imru al-Qays bin Amr, launched raids against Madhhij territories reaching Najran.

The confederation produced notable historical figures such as Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib al-Zubaidi al-Madhhaji, the legendary Yemeni knight-king who later converted to Islam, and Malik al-Ashtar al-Nakha'i, a close companion of Prophet Muhammad and military commander under Ali ibn Abi Talib. Before Islam, Madhhij maintained their own religious practices, including bringing a tribal idol to the annual pilgrimage at the Kaaba with specific ritual chants. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic conquests, particularly in campaigns against the Persian and Byzantine empires, with Prophet Muhammad reportedly stating that many people of Paradise would come from Madhhaj, praising their noble qualities of honor, bravery, justice, and wisdom.

After the establishment of Kufa as a garrison city, many Madhhij members, including Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib, settled there. The confederation later fought against the Qarmatians in Yemen under leaders like Abul Ashira and Malik ibn Marara al-Rahawi. While the original confederation structure no longer exists as a unified entity, genealogical connections to Madhhij remain important in Arabian tribal identity. Al-Hamdani's commentaries indicate that descendants still lived in the same regions he documented over 1100 years ago, demonstrating the enduring geographical continuity of Madhhij settlement patterns in Yemen and parts of Najran. Various branches of the confederation integrated into other tribal structures over time, with some branches settling in areas that later became part of modern GCC states.

folkLegends.title: The Tribal Idol at Pre-Islamic Pilgrimage
folkLegends.story: Before Islam, Madhhij had its own idol that they used to bring in the yearly pilgrimage to Kaaba. They used to make Talbiya specific to Madhhij for that idol, encircling the Kaaba several times and pleading their Madhhij Talbiya to Allah to let that idol be put around the Kaaba. The Arabs inherited the Kaaba from Ibrahim who named it Beit Allah (the house of God), but in later ages they started worshipping idols and brought them to the Kaaba to bless their idols by God.

data.history: The Maqadilah is a subsection of the Bani Shatair, one of the two main divisions of the Shihuh tribe that inhabits the northern Hajar Mountain range, specifically the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula) region of the UAE and Oman. The Bani Shatair division, which includes the Maqadilah along with the Khanabil, Kumazarah, and Mahabib subsections, is centered around Kumzar at the tip of the Musandam Peninsula, while the other main Shihuh division, the Bani Hadiyah, is mostly found on the western coast including Khasab, Oman. The Arab element of the Shihuh tribe, which forms the majority and includes the Maqadilah, is thought to be linked to the wave of immigration from Yemen that brought groups of Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century. At the turn of the 20th century, the entire Shihuh tribe numbered approximately 21,500 people, with some 14,500 settled on the coast and 7,000 inhabiting the mountainous interior, though tribal members would travel seasonally between both domains. The Shihuh, including the Maqadilah subsection, practiced a distinctive seasonal lifestyle that differed markedly from desert Bedouin culture. During winter months, they formed agricultural communities in stone-built mountain settlements, channeling water run-off to irrigate stepped fields, while in warmer months they migrated mainly to work during the date season at locations like Khasab, Dibba, and the Batinah coast of Oman, also participating in pearl fishing seasons. They maintained large herds of goats as their primary source of trade surplus. The Shihuh were historically difficult to govern and fiercely maintained their independence, frequently coming into conflict with the Al Qasimi rulers of both Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah while generally being more ready to accept the suzerainty of Muscat. Their economic needs crossed borders, with Shihuh often holding property subject to different rulers, and they played significant roles as kingmakers in settlements like Kalba and in shaping the complex territorial arrangements of towns like Dibba.

data.history: Braih (also spelled Birayh) is one of the three main divisions of the Mutayr tribal confederation, alongside Bani Abdullah and 'Ulwa. According to Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan, who are descendants of Ishmael. Modern DNA testing has confirmed that Mutayr members share the same haplogroup with most Y-chromosome lineage from the J1 Haplogroup network, supporting their common ancestry. Mutayr's original homelands were in the highlands of northern Hejaz near Medina, but in the 17th century the tribe began a large-scale eastward migration into central Arabia, displacing other Bedouin tribes such as Harb and Anizzah who were forced northward. As part of this migration, Braih became established in the Najd region where they developed a rivalry with the 'Utaybah tribe who had also moved into central Arabia from Hejaz. Because Mutayr dominated the Al-Qaseem region, which was contested between the Al Saud and Al Rashid clans in the early 20th century, the Braih and other Mutayr divisions played crucial roles in Arabian politics. Under Faisal Al-Dewish's leadership, they joined Ibn Saud's Ikhwan movement in 1912, settling in villages like al-Artawiyya and becoming warriors for the Saudi cause. The Braih participated in major military campaigns including the 1906 Battle of Rawdat Muhanna where Mutayr warriors killed Abdulaziz bin Mutaib Al Rashid, and the 1924 conquest of Hejaz. However, Al-Dewish later led an Ikhwan rebellion against Ibn Saud, which was defeated at the 1929 Battle of Sabilla. Today, the historically nomadic Braih are largely settled across Saudi Arabia's cities and towns, with significant populations also in Kuwait.

data.history: Qataban (Qatabanian: 𐩤𐩩𐩨𐩬, QTBN) was an ancient Yemenite kingdom in South Arabia that existed from the early 1st millennium BCE to the late 1st or 2nd centuries CE. It was one of the six ancient South Arabian kingdoms alongside Sabaʾ, Maʿīn, Ḥaḍramawt, Ḥimyar and Awsān. The kingdom was centered around the Wādī Bayhān with its capital at Timnaʿ, controlling territory in what is now southern Yemen and parts of modern-day Saudi Arabia. At its maximum extent, Qatabān's territory extended from the Bāb al-Mandab in the southwest to the Ṣayhad desert to the north, and the western limits of Ḥaḍramawt to the east. The earliest human occupation in the region dates to around the 20th century BCE consisting of a Neolithic population, with the earliest settlements from the 11th to 10th centuries BCE. Qatabān developed into a centralized state centered around Timnaʿ by the late 7th or early 6th century BCE, initially ruled by joint kings including Hawfiʿamm Yuhanʿim son of Sumhuʿalay Watar and Yadʿʾab son of Ḏamarʿali. In the late 7th century BCE, Qatabān initially allied with neighboring Ḥaḍramawt under Sabaean king Karibʾil Watar, but hostilities soon broke out and Qatabān came under Sabaean control during the 6th century BCE. Qatabān regained independence in the late 5th century BCE and embarked on successful expansionist policies, conquering Maʿīn and challenging Sabaean supremacy by the 3rd century BCE. The kingdom's rulers adopted prestigious titles including mukarrib ('unifier') and malik ('king'), reflecting their regional hegemony. However, Qatabān began declining in the 2nd century BCE when the Ḥimyarite tribal confederation seceded around 110 BCE and joined Sabaʾ to form the kingdom of Sabaʾ and Ḏū-Raydān. The kingdom's economy was based on irrigation agriculture, myrrh production, and most importantly, control of the lucrative frankincense trade routes connecting South Asia with the Mediterranean world. The kingdom finally ended in the late 1st century CE when Ḥaḍramawt and Ḥimyar divided and annexed its territories, marking the conclusion of over seven centuries of Qatabanian political independence.
data.nameEtymology: The name appears in ancient Qatabanian script as 𐩤𐩩𐩨𐩬 (QTBN), romanized as Qatabān. Greco-Roman sources recorded variations: Strabo referred to it as Kattabania (Κατταβανία) and Pliny the Elder called the Qatabānians the 'Gebbanitae'

The Sakasic (also spelled Sakasek) were a Himyarite tribe that emerged during the period of Himyarite dominance in southern Arabia. According to the research, they were specifically identified as one of the branches of Banu Al-Sakasek under the Kinda tribal confederation, which was part of the larger Qahtani lineage tracing back to Qahtan. The Kinda tribes, including the Sakasic, initially dwelt in Bahrain but were expelled to East Yemen, where some groups moved to Nejd and established a powerful government that served as a vassal kingdom for Himyar.

The Sakasic, alongside Banu Al-Sukun, fairly ruled parts of Hadramout in eastern Yemen during their time in the Arabian Peninsula. As a Himyarite tribe, they were part of the southern Arabian confederation that eventually conquered and incorporated the declining Sabaean kingdom by the 2nd century BC. They were contemporaries of other major Himyarite and Qahtani tribal groups including the Lakhmids and Banu Judham, with whom they would later share territories in Egypt.

Around the 3rd century AD, coinciding with the destruction of the Marib Dam and the subsequent northward migrations of many Yemeni tribes, the Sakasic migrated from their homeland in Yemen to northern Egypt. This migration was part of a larger movement of southern Arabian tribes, including the Lakhmids and Banu Judham, who were among the first Southern Arabs to settle in Northern Egypt. The Sakasic established themselves in the ancient town of Bubastis in Egypt, which they renamed Zaqaziq after their tribal name.

The Sakasic settlement became so significant that Zaqaziq not only became the modern name of the town but also gave its name to one of Egypt's provinces, demonstrating the lasting impact of their settlement in the region. Like other Kinda tribes, the Sakasic gradually declined after the fall of Himyar in 525 AD, but their legacy in Egypt through the naming of Zaqaziq and its province represents one of the most enduring examples of Arabian tribal influence in North Africa.
data.nameEtymology: The modern Egyptian city and province of Zaqaziq derives its name from the tribal name Sakasic, demonstrating the linguistic evolution from the original Arabian tribal designation to its current form

data.history: The research provided contains no direct information about Shammar Toga as a specific tribal entity. However, it does reference Shammar in the context of religious divisions, noting that the Shammar clan includes 'Sunni Al-Jarba and Shia Al-Toga branches' alongside other examples of tribal religious diversity in the region. This brief mention confirms the existence of Al-Toga (Shammar Toga) as a Shia branch of the broader Shammar confederation, distinguishing it from the Sunni Al-Jarba branch. The research focuses primarily on the Al-Daraji tribe rather than providing detailed information about Shammar Toga's specific history, genealogy, migrations, or territorial holdings. Without additional research specifically addressing Shammar Toga's origins, historical development, or role in Arabian Peninsula tribal dynamics, no further verified details can be provided about this particular tribal branch.

The Suhool (Arabic: السهول, singular Sahli) are an Arab tribe originating from the Najd region of central Saudi Arabia, descended from the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation, also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a. They are considered cousins of the Subay' tribe, sharing common Amirid lineage through this ancient tribal confederation that dominated central Arabia for centuries after the rise of Islam. The Suhool maintained the ancestral territories of their Banu 'Amir forebears, controlling lands that included Al-Ard, Al-Arid, Al-Uwairid, and Rwaygheb in the Riyadh region, with their presence extending beyond Najd to Kuwait, Iraq, Qatar, and Bahrain.

Historically, the Suhool exercised significant territorial control in central Arabia. Arab geographers and tribal historians documented that the Suhool once penetrated deep into Najd, settling in Sawad Bahla (known as 'the width') and controlling the country of Ardh Shamam. However, they later withdrew from these extensive territories, retaining only Al-Rawdah, known as Rawdah Al-Ard, as noted by Sheikh Muhammad bin Abdullah bin Blaihed and Sheikh Saad bin Junaidel.

The tribe is organized into ten major clans, each with numerous sub-branches: Al-Brazat (including Al Rashid), Al Muhaimid (Al-Maratin, Al-Sha'f), Dhahran (Al-Suwaidan, Al-Hadha, Al-Zayed, Al-Munikher, Al-Fatamin, Al-Abboud), Al-Zaqa'in (Al-Ruwaidan, Al-Thunayan, Al-Khudair, Al-Shalhoub, Al-Damkh, Al-Hamadin, Al-Saif, Al-Tarjam, Al-Qanaat), the Sa'ub family (Al Mudheesh, Al Ali, Al Mufreh, Al Muqass), Al Obaid (Al Qatian, Al Jarboa), Al-Qabbaneh (Al-Shakhatla, Al-Muharka, Al-Drivers, Al-Ghaliz, Al-Hawazma, Al-Jalal, Al-Jariba, Al-Zaid, Al-Anaqid, Al-Theeb), Al-Mahaniyah (Al-Fadl, Al-Faleh, Al-Hamoud, Al-Flayh, Al-Arefaj), the Juror clan (Al Hoymel, Al Razaan, Al Ateeq, Abu Jada), and Al Manjal (Al Mufarrej, Al Alyan, Al Al-Walah).

In 1873, the Suhool demonstrated their continued political significance by participating alongside the Ajman, Mutair, Subay', and Dawasir tribes in supporting Imam Saud bin Faisal Al Saud during the Second Battle of Talal against Muslit bin Rubayan. Despite fielding 3,000 fighters against Muslit's 800 men, this campaign ended in defeat for the Imam's forces, marking a decisive moment in the political history of central Arabia and illustrating the complex tribal alliances that characterized 19th-century Arabian politics.

The Tunaij (Arabic: الطنيج, singular Al Tunaiji الطنيجي) is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates that has been linked to the Bani Qitab. By the turn of the 19th century, approximately 4,000 Tunaij inhabited the Northern Emirates, with 1,500 being Bedouin, making them influential in tribal politics due to their ability to raise a force of 500 fighting men. The tribe primarily settled in three locations: the inland oasis town of Dhaid in Sharjah, where the Bedouin Tunaij established their center with around 70 houses protected by a fortified tower; the coastal town of Rams in Ras Al Khaimah, where all 400 houses were settled by the Tunaij by the early 19th century; and small numbers at Hamriyah.

The Tunaij of Rams were primarily involved in pearl fishing, operating a fleet of 3 pearl boats and 10 fishing boats. During the pearling season, both Bedouin Tunaij from the interior and members of the mountain Shihuh tribe would come to the coast to work as seasonal laborers on Tunaij pearling expeditions. The tribe maintained dual economic strategies - the Bedouin section based at Dhaid focused on pastoralism and date cultivation, while the coastal Tunaij at Rams specialized in maritime activities.

The Tunaij became significantly involved in the 1819 British punitive expedition against the Qawasim. When British forces landed at Rams on December 18, 1819, and fought their way inland through date plantations to Dhayah Fort, many Tunaij herders and farmers from the date groves fled to the hilltop fortification for protection. The Tunaij formed the majority of the 400 people who held out in Dhayah Fort for three days under heavy bombardment from British mortars and cannons, enduring conditions without proper sanitation, water, or effective cover from the sun.

After the British brought up two 24-pound cannons from HMS Liverpool and subjected the fort to sustained bombardment that breached its walls, the defenders finally surrendered at 10:30 AM on December 22, 1819. Of the 400 people who surrendered, only 177 were identified as men of fighting age, indicating that the majority were civilian refugees including women, children, and elderly. This resistance was part of the broader conflict that led to the General Maritime Treaty of 1820, with Hassan bin Ali, identified as the 'Sheikh of Zyah' (actually Sheikh of Rams and Dhayah), becoming a co-signatory of the treaty that established the foundations of what would become the United Arab Emirates.

data.history:
The Yidwah are a subsection of the Bani Kaab tribe, an Arab tribal confederation prominent in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab, including the Yidwah, are associated with territories around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and areas east of Buraimi including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Among the various Bani Kaab subsections - which include the Drisah, Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Shwaihiyin, Yidwah and Zahairat - the Yidwah were among the settled sections by the turn of the 20th century, distinguishing them from the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin subsections.

In 1844, the Yidwah, as part of the Bani Kaab confederation, allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nayhan of the Bani Yas in a tribal coalition that successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. This alliance was part of a broader confederation of interior southeastern Arabian tribes united against Wahhabi expansion. The Bani Kaab, including the Yidwah, were rivals to the Na'im and Bani Qitab tribes, having pushed sections of the Na'im north as far as the Jiri plain.

Tribal alliances in southeastern Arabia proved fluid over time. By the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had shifted to supporting Saudi interests, with their sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring to Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province Governor that 'Our territories are yours.' During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1950s, the Bani Kaab, including the Yidwah subsection, supported the Saudi incursion under Shaikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and fought against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies. By the turn of the 20th century, the settled sections of Bani Kaab, including the Yidwah, comprised part of a population of some 7,250, of whom 1,150 remained Bedouin.

data.history:
Banu Asad is an ancient and influential Arab tribe descended from Asad ibn Khuzaymah of the Adnanite lineage. Their full genealogy traces through Asad ibn Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them cousins of Prophet Muhammad through their shared ancestor Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah. The tribe gained prominence in the 6th century when they successfully revolted against the Kingdom of Kindah, killing King Hujr (father of the famous poet Imru' al-Qais) and contributing to the kingdom's ultimate collapse after a prolonged war.

In the 7th century, Banu Asad migrated to Iraq and settled primarily in Kufa near the banks of the Euphrates River, with additional settlements in Basra and Ahvaz. During the early Islamic period, the tribe aligned with Ali ibn Abi Talib in the Battle of the Camel and played a crucial role in the Battle of Karbala (680 CE). Many tribal members died alongside Husayn ibn Ali, including prominent figures like Habib ibn Muzahir (commander of the left flank), Muslim ibn Awsaja al-Asadi, and others. The tribe gained lasting honor among Shia Muslims by burying the bodies of Husayn and his companions three days after the massacre on the 13th of Muharram, with the help of Ali ibn Husayn Zayn al-Abidin.

In 998 CE, Ali ben Mazyad established the virtually independent Mazyadid emirate in the Kufa area, backed by a powerful tribal army. The Mazyadids enjoyed great influence for over a century, acquiring titles and subsidies from the Buyids in return for military services. Their most lasting achievement was founding Hillah in 1101, which became their capital and remains one of Iraq's main cities. The dynasty was known for their generosity to poets and scholars, with historical sources praising their role as 'refuge of those who were in need' and 'shelter for the expectants.'

Today, Banu Asad members are primarily found in Iraqi cities including Baghdad, Basra, Najaf, Kufa, Karbala, Nasiriyah, Amarah, Kut, Hillah, and Diyala. The tribe has several branches outside Iraq: Banu Kahil in Northern Sudan (migrated from Hijaz), communities in Iran's Khuzestan province (Ahvaz) alongside Banu Tamim and other Arab tribes, families in Kuwait (including the Al-Mazeedi who played important roles in Kuwait's constitutional development), branches in Yemen's Hadhramaut region, Oman, and even India's Karnataka state where Persian-speaking members arrived during Tipu Sultan's rule and established the Thokur Jamia Masjid in Mangalore. Modern family names include Assadi, Al-Assadi, and Al-Mazeedi, with both Sunni and Shia branches represented across different regions.
